system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 7: Study 7 - Autumn Leaves
Pages 240-279 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

The Dominant Bebop Scale

Although there are several kinds of bebop scales, the dominant bebop scale is what is normally
referred to as “The Bebop Scale”.

The dominant bebop scale is a Mixolydian mode with a descending chromatic note between the root
and the b7.

| (Dominant) bebop scale = Mixolydian mode + 7

Here is a comparison between the Mixolydian mode and the dominant bebop scale.
I reversed the scale in the diagram because that is how the bebop scale is usually played.

The note that is added to the G Mixolydian scale is the 7 (F#).

G Mixolydian Scale (reversed)

G Bebop Scale (reversed)

The G dominant bebop scale can be played over:

¢ G7, G9, G13

e Gsus4

e Dm7 (in a II VI in C major)

e Bm7b5 (in a II VI in A minor)

The Minor Bebop Scale

The minor bebop scale is a Dorian mode with a descending chromatic note between the root and the
b7.

| Minor bebop scale = Dorian mode + 7

Here is the formula for the minor bebop scale and a comparison with the Dorian mode:

D Dorian Scale (reversed)

D Minor Bebop Scale (reversed)

The D minor bebop scale can be played over Dm7, Dm6, Dm9, Dm 11, ...

Here are the two most common fingerings for the D minor bebop scale:

The Major Bebop Scale

The major bebop scale is a major scale with a descending chromatic note between the 6th and the
5th.

| Major bebop scale = major scale + b6

Here is the formula of the major bebop scale and a comparison with the major scale:

C Major Scale (reversed)

C Major Bebop Scale (reversed)

The C major bebop scale can be played over Cmaj7, C6, Cmaj9, C6/9, ...

Here are the two most common fingerings for the C major bebop scale:

Pattern 61 - Dm7 (Bebop Melodic Minor Scale)

Pattern 61 is built with the D bebop melodic minor scale, a combination between the melodic minor
scale and the major bebop scale.

| Bebop melodic minor scale = melodic minor scale + b6

Pattern 63 — Short II V (Diminished Sub)

Pattern 63 uses a diminished arpeggio over the dominant chord (see pattern 49).
Also notice the voice leading going from the 5th of Dm7, to the b9 of G7, to the 5th of Cmaj7.

This is a common voice leading over a II V I.

Pattern 69 —- Minor II V I (Harmonic Minor)

The Bm7b5 arpeggio in this pattern has a chromatic movement between the b5 and b3.

Over the E7, I start with the A harmonic minor scale (see pattern 45) and continue with an Fdim7
arpeggio (see pattern 49).

Pattern 70 —- Dm/maj7 (Augmented Sub)

Patterns 4 and 35 featured a major augmented arpeggio over a dominant chord.

Major augmented can also be used over minor chords and results in a minor/major 7 sound.

Over a minor chord, you can play an augmented triad or augmented major 7 arpeggio, both
built on the b3 of the minor chord.

For example: F aug or Fmaj7#5 over Dm7
